meta.title: libvrf — Verifiable Random Functions for JavaScript
meta.description: RFC 9381 RSA-FDH/PSS VRF for Node.js; EC VRF for browsers. TypeScript port of Microsoft libvrf.

hero.label: Verifiable randomness
hero.title:
Random you can prove.
Verification anyone can run.
hero.lead: A Verifiable Random Function maps a secret key and public input α to a unique output β and a proof π. Only the key holder produces π; anyone with the public key verifies it—in milliseconds.

pipeline.title: VRF evaluation pipeline
pipeline.sub: One input α → proof π → verified output β

concept.label: Concept
concept.title: What is a Verifiable Random Function?
concept.lead: A VRF is a function β = fSK(α) where only someone who knows the secret key SK can compute β and a proof π. Anyone with the public key PK can check π quickly and recover the same β.
concept.card1Text: β looks random to anyone without π. Same (SK, α) always gives the same β—useful for deterministic lotteries and leader election.
concept.card2Text: The prover sends π with α. Verifiers do not need SK and do not redo the expensive derivation.
concept.card3Text: Validators, contracts, or clients confirm correctness with PK only—no trust in the prover’s hardware.
concept.whoHtml:
Prover — SK, runs getVRFProof
Verifier — PK, runs verifyVRFProof
Everyone — agrees on input α
concept.rfcHtml:
RFC 9381 RSA-FDH & RSA-PSS-NOSALT (Microsoft libvrf interop)

Simplified EC P-256 in browsers — not RFC wire-compatible

uses.label: Use cases
uses.title: Where VRFs are used
uses.lead: Protocols need unpredictable, agreed-upon randomness with evidence that nobody cheated.
uses.flowSub: VRF output β picks a proposer; π proves the draw was fair
uses.uc1Text: Prove deterministic responses tied to zone keys (RSA suites in this library match RFC 9381).
uses.uc2Text: Combine VRF outputs across rounds for public randomness with audit trails.
uses.uc3Text: Commit to α, reveal β with π so players can verify the draw was not rigged.

library.label: libvrf
library.title: This library
library.lead: TypeScript port of Microsoft libvrf — RSA in Node.js, EC in browsers via WebCrypto.
library.rsaFdhText: RFC 9381 full-domain hash. 2048–4096 bit keys, SHA-256/384/512. Cross-tested vs C++ vectors.
library.rsaPssText: RFC 9381 PSS variant without salt. Same key sizes; interop with libvrf wire format.
library.ecText: Simplified EC VRF for demos & apps. Use async APIs; not RFC ECVRF wire format.
library.callout: Production: Prefer RSA suites on Node for RFC 9381 interop. Use the live demo to learn SK / PK / π / β, then deploy RSA in your backend.

demo.label: Interactive demo
demo.title: EC VRF in your browser
demo.lead: Runs EC_VRF_P256_SHA256_TAI with WebCrypto. RSA suites need Node.js — see the home page.
demo.runLead: A VRF takes a public input α and a secret key SK. Only the holder of SK can produce a proof π. Anyone with the public key PK can check π and learn the VRF output β — a deterministic pseudorandom value for that α.
demo.glossSkText: Private material generated in your browser. It never leaves this page unless you export it. Who can use it: only the prover — to call getVRFProofAsync.
demo.glossPkText: Derived from SK. Safe to publish. Identifies which prover family you trust. Who can use it: verifiers — to call verifyVRFProofAsync.
demo.glossAlphaText: Any byte string (here, text you type). Same α + same SK always yields the same β and π. Different α → different unpredictable β.
demo.glossProofText: Evidence that β was computed correctly from α under PK. Send π to a verifier along with α; they do not need SK.
demo.glossBetaText: The pseudorandom value returned after successful verification (from getVRFValue() on the proof). Behaves like a random oracle to outsiders who have not seen π.
demo.glossRfcText: This demo uses a simplified EC construction for browsers. Wire format and β differ from Microsoft libvrf / RFC 9381 ECVRF. RSA-FDH/PSS in the npm package are RFC 9381 aligned (Node only).
demo.tryLead: Each click generates a new random SK (fresh key pair). In production you would reuse one SK for many inputs.
demo.fieldHint: Public message — verifiers will need the same bytes
demo.logPlaceholder: Press the button above. Lines appear here as the VRF runs.
demo.resSkDesc: Ephemeral P-256 private key — proves you know SK by producing π.
demo.resPkDesc: Compressed EC point — share this so others can verify your proofs.
demo.resAlphaDesc: The challenge everyone agrees on — changing one character changes β.
demo.resProofDesc: Serialized proof bytes — attach to α when sending to a verifier.
demo.resBetaDesc: Returned only if verification succeeds. Use β as lottery seed, leader election hash, etc.

demoRun.bundleMissing: libvrf bundle not found. Build the site with: npm run website:build
demoRun.inputMean: Input α — public challenge. Everyone must use the same bytes to verify. Not secret.
demoRun.creatingSkMean: Secret key (SK) — private P-256 key. Only the prover holds this. Used in getVRFProofAsync.
demoRun.derivingPkMean: Public key (PK) — derived from SK. Safe to publish. Verifiers need PK, not SK.
demoRun.provingMean: Proof π — computed with SK + α. Proves a valid β for this input without revealing SK.
demoRun.verifyingMean: Verify — checks π against PK and α. No secret key needed. Returns success + VRF output β.
demoRun.verifyFail: Verification failed — π does not match α under this PK
demoRun.verifyOk: Yes — π is valid for this α
demoRun.verifiedMean: Success — π is valid. The verifier accepts that β was produced correctly from α under PK.
demoRun.timingMean: Timing — prove needs SK (slower crypto); verify only checks π (usually faster).
demoRun.proofSizeMean: Proof π (size) — wire bytes you would send to a verifier alongside α.
demoRun.betaMean: VRF output β — deterministic pseudorandom hash for this (SK, α). Same inputs → same β. Use as seed / lottery / leader election.
